title: API Design Review
description: Use when reviewing REST, GraphQL, RPC, or webhook API contracts for naming, versioning, error semantics, compatibility, auth, idempotency, pagination, or long-term client safety.

input_schema.api_spec: string — API contract in any format: OpenAPI 3.x, GraphQL schema, or raw endpoint definitions with method/path/body/response
input_schema.constraints: string — compatibility requirements, latency SLA, auth model, governance rules (e.g. 'Must support v1 clients for 6 months, p99 < 200ms, OAuth 2.0 bearer')

api_surface_types.REST resource: Review resource model, HTTP semantics, status codes, pagination, and idempotency.
api_surface_types.GraphQL: Review schema evolution, nullability, resolver cost, pagination, and deprecation.
api_surface_types.RPC/action: Review command names, request/response shape, retry semantics, and deadlines.
api_surface_types.Webhook/event: Review delivery guarantees, schema versioning, idempotency, ordering, and retries.
api_surface_types.SDK/CLI/config: Review naming, defaults, backwards compatibility, and error surfaces.

change_risk_classes.Additive: New optional field, endpoint, enum value clients can ignore, or new event type.
change_risk_classes.Behavior-changing: Same shape but different meaning, timing, sorting, default, authorization, or side effect.
change_risk_classes.Breaking: Removal, rename, tightened validation, requiredness/nullability change, or changed error/status contract.
change_risk_classes.Operational: Same contract but different rate limits, retryability, timeout, consistency, or ordering.

instructions: Classify API surface type and change risk class before listing findings.

Build a contract inventory covering resources, operations, request and response shapes, auth context, pagination, filtering, sorting, async behavior, SDK/CLI surfaces, events/webhooks, and documented errors.
Review the surface-specific concerns for REST, GraphQL, RPC/action, webhook/event, or SDK/CLI/config; do not apply REST-only rules blindly.
Review naming and resource/action modeling against the existing API style; flag inconsistent pluralization, verbs in resource paths, hidden side effects, and unclear ownership boundaries.
Check method/action semantics and safety; safe reads must not mutate, mutating retries must document idempotency keys or replay behavior, and bulk operations must define partial failure handling.
Validate response and error consistency across status codes, envelopes, machine-readable error codes, retryability, validation details, correlation IDs, rate-limit headers, and empty/list responses.
Classify compatibility risk as additive, behavior-changing, breaking, or operational; state migration path, deprecation/versioning mechanism, and client notice period.

Review operational contract details including authz/authn boundary, tenant isolation, pagination stability, ordering guarantees, limits, timeouts, eventual consistency, webhook retries, and observability fields.
Order findings by severity and include affected contract element, surface type, risk class, client impact, fix, compatibility impact, and before/after examples when shapes change.
Do not recommend a breaking change without a migration or versioning plan.

examples.input: REST spec for user lifecycle: PUT /users/{id} replaces entire user object; errors return {message: string} on some endpoints and {error: {code, detail}} on others.
examples.output:
High: PUT /users/{id} currently behaves like partial update and silently drops omitted fields, so retries and SDK clients can lose data. Fix: use PATCH /users/{id} for partial updates; keep PUT only for full replacement with required-field validation. Compatibility: additive if PUT behavior is preserved during a deprecation window.
Medium: Error schema alternates between {message} and {error}. Fix: normalize to {error: {code: string, message: string, details?: object}}; document stable machine codes.
Open Question: confirm whether existing clients already depend on unversioned paths before adding /v1.
